the_good: Good product, good pay. Started RM3000, bumped to RM4500 within 3 months.
For context: 2 years later pay was only RM5000 — Shopcada paid well early.
First year was solid. Boss loved me. Everyone loved me.
Genuinely loved the place and the people.
what_went_wrong: After the first year, got lazy and started slacking. Gave bare minimum. Work quality dropped.
Had a wrong assumption: thought work culture runs on silent slacking. Was wrong.
Work culture never made sense — who sells 8 hours a day for 60 years? — but the assumption that others were coasting too was incorrect.
Boss eventually hated me. He had anger issues but was generally reasonable.
Listening to friends now, what I did wasn't that bad compared to what they do — and they don't seem punished as badly. But still own the mistake.
fair_criticism_of_the_company: No code review at all. Code just passed through.
Tech lead (Shiau Shin / SS) was not strong. No push and pull, pure blind trust in the junior.
No comments on poor work performance. Daily standup every morning: what I did yesterday, what I plan today — but if the kanban board never moved, no problem.
Boss noticed only when things were already a point of no return.
From current view: letting code pass without review is irresponsible. Every output of everyone under you is your output.
interview_frame: My career started okay but went downhill from my own mistakes. From Shopcada, I learned to take my work seriously.
real_work: Built and maintained shipping integrations: J&T, NinjaVan, ParcelHub
Built and maintained marketplace integrations: Shopee, Lazada, Zalora
These were core features — the operational backbone for key clients
Built a real-time sales dashboard for Facebook Live streams using webhooks and WebSockets, surfacing live order and revenue data during streams
Boss loved the FB live dashboard. From current view it was naive junior work, but still okay considering no AI to help.
Day to day: kanban board, fix bugs, occasional new feature, add new integration
notes: ADD MORE MEMORIES HERE AS THEY COME BACK
